questions.question: Explain the difference between a leaf function and a non-leaf function in ARM. How does this affect the return mechanism?
keyPoints: Leaf function does not call other functions; non-leaf function calls other functions
Leaf function can return using MOV PC, LR
Non-leaf function must PUSH {LR} at start and POP {PC} at end
Non-leaf functions need to save LR because it gets overwritten by nested BL calls

questions.question: List and explain the register usage conventions according to AAPCS in ARM.
keyPoints: R0-R3: Argument registers (first 4 parameters) and return value register
R4-R11: Callee-saved registers - must be preserved by called function
R12: Caller-saved scratch register
R13 (SP): Stack Pointer
R14 (LR): Link Register for function calls
R15 (PC): Program Counter

questions.question: Describe the sequence of operations that occur when a function is called in ARM Cortex-M.
keyPoints: Caller places first 4 arguments in R0-R3, additional on stack
BL instruction executes, saving return address in LR
Function prolog: PUSH {registers} to save callee-saved registers and LR
Function body executes with parameters in R0-R3
Return value placed in R0 (or R0-R1 for 64-bit)
Function epilog: POP {registers} to restore saved values
Return via MOV PC, LR (leaf) or POP {PC} (non-leaf)

questions.question: What is the purpose of the Frame Pointer? When is it typically used?
keyPoints: Frame Pointer (R11/FP) provides stable reference to stack frame
It allows easy access to parameters and local variables at fixed offsets
Useful for debugging - easier to locate variables in stack dump
Used when variable-length local data or complex stack allocation exists
Establishes stack frame with PUSH {R11, LR} and PUSH {R11, SP} to save old FP

questions.question: Explain how parameters are passed to functions with more than 4 arguments in ARM.
keyPoints: First 4 parameters passed in R0, R1, R2, R3
5th and subsequent parameters passed on the stack
Stack parameters pushed in reverse order (rightmost first)
Stack cleanup responsibility depends on calling convention
Parameters on stack accessed using FP or SP with appropriate offsets

questions.question: Write ARM assembly code for a function prolog and epilog of a non-leaf function that uses registers R4 and R5.
keyPoints: Prolog should PUSH {R4, R5, LR} or PUSH {R4-R5, LR}
Epilog should POP {R4-R5, PC} in reverse order
Show proper stack alignment considerations
Include comments explaining each instruction's purpose

questions.question: What is STMFD instruction and how is it used in function calls?
keyPoints: STMFD = Store Multiple Full Descending
Decrements SP first, then stores registers
Used to save multiple registers to stack efficiently
Example: STMFD SP!, {R4-R7, LR} saves 5 registers
The ! indicates write-back to SP (auto-update)

questions.question: Write a complete ARM assembly program demonstrating function call with parameters and return value. Include a main function that calls a function to add two numbers.
keyPoints: Define main function with PUSH {LR} at start
Load parameters into R0 and R1 before BL
Implement add function: save registers if needed, perform addition in R0
Return using MOV PC, LR for leaf function
Main function retrieves return value from R0
Include proper function prolog/epilog
Explain each step in comments

questions.question: Explain the complete process of a nested function call in ARM. Write assembly code to demonstrate a function A calling function B.
keyPoints: Explain nested call: A calls B while A hasn't completed
Show how LR gets overwritten when B is called
Demonstrate saving LR to stack before nested call
Explain that each nested level saves LR to stack
Write code showing function A: PUSH {LR}, then BL B, then POP {PC}
Show return flow: B returns to A, A returns to caller
Explain stack growth during nesting

questions.question: Design and explain a factorial function in ARM assembly (recursive implementation). Show how the stack is used to preserve return addresses and intermediate results.
keyPoints: Base case: if n<=1, return 1 in R0
Recursive case: save LR and n to stack
Call factorial with (n-1)
Multiply result by current n
Restore saved values and return
Show stack frame contents at each recursion level
Explain how LR allows correct return to each recursive call

questions.question: Compare the caller-saved and callee-saved register conventions with examples. When would you choose to use one over the other?
keyPoints: Caller-saved: R0-R3, R12 - caller must save if needed after call
Callee-saved: R4-R11 - called function must preserve
Example showing caller saving before and after call
Example showing callee saving at function entry
Trade-offs: caller-saved for leaf functions, callee-saved for non-leaf
Performance considerations: minimizing saves/restores
